data.title: SocGen Reiterates Commitment to Net-Zero Banking Alliance
data.description:
Société Générale will remain a member of the Net-Zero Banking Alliance, Bloomberg News reported Thursday, adding that the French company is committed to reducing its emissions.

Chief Executive Officer Slawomir Krupa said at a press conference that the French bank's membership in the program helps it better understand its direct and indirect emissions while offering "real economic opportunity" to clients.

Several US-based banks have recently left the United Nations-launched Net-Zero Banking Alliance.

data.title: APCU/Center Parc Credit Union Adopts Point Predictive's IEValidate™ to Transform Member Income and Employment Documentation Requirements
data.description:
SAN DIEGO, Feb. 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Point Predictive, the leader in risk solutions for consumer lending, today announced that Atlanta-based APCU/Center Parc Credit Union has implemented IEValidate, its innovative income and employment validation solution. This strategic adoption aims to streamline the lending process for members while strengthening the credit union's fraud prevention capabilities.

In an era where traditional income verification methods often create friction in the lending process, APCU/Center Parc recognized the need for a more efficient solution. IEValidate provides their underwriters with a frictionless way to validate member income and employment, eliminating the reliance on traditional paystubs which can be both difficult to evaluate and susceptible to forgery.

The solution offers several key advantages for the credit union:

  • Eliminates paystubs, streamlining 80% or more of loans.
  • Taps into insights from over 300 million reported incomes.
  • Screens against 22 million reported employers and over 14,000 fake employers.
  • Delivers income and employment evaluation on 100% of loan requests.

"Point Predictive is fundamentally changing how income and employment are validated in the lending industry," said Tim Grace, CEO of Point Predictive. "Traditional methods like paystubs are not only burdensome for members, but they are also easily forged making paystub reviews both time consuming and ineffective. Our collaboration with APCU/Center Parc, will enable them to instantly validate both income and employment on every single application without holding up the lending process by requesting paystubs or bank statements.”

The implementation of IEValidate gives APCU/Center Parc access to Point Predictive's proprietary data repository, which includes over 300 million income reports and information on 22 million employers. This comprehensive database, combined with sophisticated validation processes, enables the credit union to make faster, more confident lending decisions without requiring members to provide traditional documentation.

"IEValidate allows us to enhance our member experience by removing unnecessary friction from the loan application process," said Blake Graham, President/CEO of APCU/Center Parc. "The solution provides our underwriters with clear, objective information they need to make confident decisions, replacing the subjective nature of traditional paystub review with reliable, data-driven insights."

Early adopters of IEValidate have reported doubling their loan conversion rates when eliminating paystub requirements for low-risk applications, while maintaining strong portfolio performance.

For more information, contact info@pointpredictive.com.

About Point Predictive

Point Predictive powers a new level of lending confidence and speed through artificial intelligence, powerful data insight from our proprietary data repository, and decades of risk management expertise. The company's data and technology solutions quickly and accurately identify truthful and untruthful disclosures on loan applications. As a result, lenders can fund the majority of loans without requiring onerous documentation, such as paystubs, utility bills, or bank statements, improving funding rates while reducing early payment default losses. Subsequently, borrowers get loans faster, and lenders realize a more profitable bottom line.

About Atlanta Postal Credit Union

At APCU/Center Parc, providing our members first-class service has always been our priority. We're a member-owned, not-for-profit financial cooperative committed to helping hardworking people save money and prosper. For 100 years, we've stayed true to these beliefs. As Georgia's oldest credit union, we're proud of our tradition of service. Over the years, APCU/Center Parc assets have grown from an initial investment of $2,505 to more than $2.3 billion. Today, we're one of the largest credit unions in the country. We're proud to serve more than 108,000 members nationwide with a full complement of financial products and services designed to save them money. For more information, visit www.apcu.com. This credit union is federally insured by the National Credit Union Administration.

Media Contact

Jill Robb

JROBB@PointPredictive.com

data.title: Lyft Collaborating With Alphabet-Backed Anthropic to Develop AI Products for Riders, Drivers
data.description:
Lyft said Thursday it is collaborating with Alphabet-backed (GOOG, GOOGL) Anthropic to develop artificial-intelligence products to improve the rideshare experience.

The first phase of the collaboration is already underway with Lyft incorporating Anthropic's Claude via Amazon (AMZN) Bedrock into its customer-care AI assistant, Lyft said.

data.title: Mining Companies Seen Able to Absorb Near-Term Hit From Any Tariffs — Market Talk
data.description: Natural resources companies should weather the tariff storm if imposed, Morningstar DBRS suggests. Although U.S. tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico have been delayed, the threat remains. Morningstar says there would be short-term disruptions in trade flows until supply chains are rebalanced if the levies are imposed. Yet it doesn't expect mining companies to scale back production and would instead deliver on operational guidance provided for 2025. The robust commodity prices that have persisted over the past few years have allowed mining companies to significantly reduce debt levels and consequently they have strong credit metrics, Morningstar says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0955 ET - Natural resources companies should weather the tariff storm if imposed, Morningstar DBRS suggests. Although U.S. tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico have been delayed, the threat remains. Morningstar says there would be short-term disruptions in trade flows until supply chains are rebalanced if the levies are imposed. Yet it doesn't expect mining companies to scale back production and would instead deliver on operational guidance provided for 2025. The robust commodity prices that have persisted over the past few years have allowed mining companies to significantly reduce debt levels and consequently they have strong credit metrics, Morningstar says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0950 ET - Oil futures are recouping some of the previous day's losses, while the market continues to ponder the impact of trade tariffs, rising U.S. crude stocks, and prospects of a step-up in OPEC+ output in April. "Most traders feel the U.S. tariff threats to China are for real and could damage their economy further, and with OPEC set to raise production soon, WTI prices may have heavier headwinds in the future," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Look for a choppy type trade until more is known about tariffs." WTI is up 0.7% at $71.53 a barrel, and Brent is 0.6% higher at $75.02.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0903 ET - Sterling's appreciation potential against the dollar looks limited after the Bank of England's latest policy decision, J.P. Morgan Private Bank strategist Matthew Landon says in a note. The BOE cut rates by 25 basis points to 4.5% Thursday but two of the nine members preferred a 50 basis-point reduction. The vote probably reflects concerns over a weaker growth outlook, Landon says. The market is now pricing the most rate cuts for this year since November's U.S. presidential election, he says. Given how weak growth is, greater policy divergence with the Federal Reserve seems justified. This will "likely keep a ceiling on how far the pound can appreciate against the dollar over the near-term." Sterling falls 0.9% to $1.2382. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0858 ET - Gold futures fall, but keep close to record highs on macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty. Futures are down 0.4% at $2,881.10 a troy ounce, after hitting a fresh record of $2,906 an ounce in the prior session. The precious metal is keeping near highs amid trade war concerns, ING analysts say in a note. The risk of economic conflict is risking higher inflation and slower growth, spurring demand for safe-haven assets, ING says. While trade and tariff uncertainty continue to boost precious metal prices, U.S. President Trump's latest comments that the U.S. should take over the Gaza strip and assume responsibility for reconstructing the territory have added to gold's bullish momentum, ING says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0744 ET - Eurozone households are keeping their purse-strings tight amid a global economic maelstrom, ING economist Bert Colijn says. Retail sales fell back at the end of the year in the currency area, reversing some of the gains made earlier in the year, figures show Thursday. That weakness is down to cautious sentiment among consumers spooked by the election of Donald Trump in the U.S., Colijn says. Underyling improvement in the European economy should support spending ahead, he says. "But the question is how gloomy consumers remain in the months ahead despite incomes improving," Colijn tells investors in a note.((joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby))

0643 ET - The White House's tariffs on China and potential tariffs on Canada has Canadian businesses worried about their supply chains, research by KPMG shows. A survey of 250 business leaders across the county finds 88% of Canadian businesses have diverted or are considering diverting goods to countries not facing tariffs. And 44% say they are already reconfiguring their supply chains to divert U.S.-destined exports to these third-party countries, while another 44% are exploring that option. KPMG says 63% of respondents expect their business will be negatively affected by the tariffs on Chinese goods. Uncertainty over U.S. policy is high, and 79% of respondent say they expect the U.S. will impose tariffs on the European Union. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0637 ET - Bitcoin rises after President Trump's son hinted at an investment in the cryptocurrency, Saxo Bank analysts say in a note. Eric Trump said it "feels like a great time to enter bitcoin" in a post on the X social media platform. He also linked the Trump-backed World Liberty Financial, a decentralized finance initiative centered around a crypto token called WLFI. Overall the crypto market remains resilient despite macroeconomic pressures, the analysts say. Bitcoin rises 2.2% to $99,028, according to LSEG.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0253 ET - The dollar edges higher as uncertainty around President Trump's trade policy remains elevated. Safe-haven demand should persist for the time being, with market participants seeking shelter in the dollar as a result of ongoing policy uncertainties, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. Trump's tariff plans will have a less detrimental impact on the U.S. economy compared to developed market peers, he says. This effectively puts the dollar in a position of being "the best of a bad bunch." The DXY dollar index rises 0.2% to 107.768 after hitting a one-week low of 107.296 Wednesday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0222 ET - The FTSE 100 is expected to open 73 points higher, or 0.9%, according to IG. The index closed 52 points higher, or 0.6%, to 8523 points Wednesday. Market sentiment across Europe and the U.S. has improved on waning trade tensions with President Trump, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. Investors are also looking ahead to the latest policy decision from the Bank of England at 1200 GMT. The BOE is expected to lower interest rates by 25 basis points as U.K. Treasury chief Rachel Reeves's growth plans are "clouded by the country's restricted finances and the economy calls for BOE support," she says.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1829 ET - Ford would be able to manage a few weeks of President Trump's proposed tariffs given the rate and flow of its products, CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts. If they persisted, though, they would have a devastating impact, he says. The company already has plans to expand some of its U.S. plants to mitigate higher import costs, but if the proposed tariffs become a long-term reality, Ford would have to make some major strategy shifts, such as building all new plants in the U.S., Farley says. Shares fall 5.1% after hours. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1817 ET - Ares Management expects that some of the policies of the new Trump administration will foster dealmaking, giving a further boost to what the private-credit fund manager sees as an already favorable market for investments, Ares CEO Michael Arougheti says during an earnings call. "We expect transaction activity to expand year-over-year," he says. "The new administration could provide some tailwinds for deployment, especially if an improved regulatory and antitrust environment enables more business growth and [mergers and acquisitions] activity in the market." Ares ended December with $133 billion in dry powder, or capital available to spend, a record for the firm. (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1814 ET - Ford CEO Jim Farley says on a call with analysts that President Trump's proposed tariffs could hurt the U.S. auto industry. "There's no question that tariffs at the 25% level from Canada and Mexico, if they're protracted, would have a huge impact on our industry, with billions of dollars of industry profits wiped out and adverse effects on U.S. jobs," he says. Consumers would also face higher prices, he adds. Ford applauds Trump's administration for the agreements with Mexico and Canada earlier this week and says it is closely monitoring the situation in China. Shares fall 5.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Buckle Says Comparable Store Net Sales Rise in January
data.description:
Buckle said Thursday that for the four-week period ended Feb. 1, comparable net sales for stores open at least one year grew 4.4% from a year earlier.

Net sales for the four-week fiscal month ended Feb. 1 declined 18% to $59.5 million, compared with net sales of $72.6 million for the five-week fiscal month ended Feb. 3, 2024.

The company also said comparable store net sales for the 13-week quarter ended Feb. 1 were up 3.9% from a year earlier, while net sales for the 13-week fiscal quarter ended Feb. 1 were down 0.8% from a year earlier.

For the 52-week fiscal year ended Feb. 1, Buckle said comparable store net sales were down 2.7% from a year earlier, while net sales for the same period declined 3.4% to $1.22 billion, compared with the 53-week fiscal year ended Feb. 3, 2024.

Shares of Buckle were up 1.5% in recent trading.

data.title: Mercado Libre CEO and Founder Marcos Galperin Reflects on the company's 25-Year Journey and DNA in Latest Episode of Inside Mercado Libre
data.description:
Montevideo, Uruguay, 6 February 2025, Feb. 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Mercado Libre , the leading e-commerce and fintech platform in Latin America, drops the fifth episode of its podcast series, Inside Mercado Libre, "Marcos Galperin on MELI's Culture” featuring its CEO and founder Marcos Galperin. The Inside Mercado Libre podcast, hosted by Investor Relations Officer, Richard Cathcart, provides investors with unique access to the company executives’ strategic views. 

In this special episode Marcos Galperin reflects on the company’s 25-year journey and culture that have fueled its transformation into a Latin American powerhouse —hard work, meritocracy, excellence, entrepreneurship and long-term focus—and how it has shaped a dynamic and competitive ecosystem. He also discusses the team’s ambitions, emphasizing that despite their achievements, there is no room for complacency as they strive to further expand their impact across Latin America. 

For Galperin, “there's still so much to do, there's still so many opportunities and the fact that we're willing to take risks and make big bets to continue growing and the fact that we're growing at the same rates that we were growing 25 years ago is amazing.” The team, of over 85,000 talented employees across Latin America, continuously evaluates threats, explores new business opportunities, shares and learns from mistakes and successes and strives to do better. This teamwork ensures Mercado Libre remains adaptable and competitive in a rapidly evolving industry.

Listen to the latest episode "Marcos Galperin on MELI's Culture” on Spotify. Previous episodes “Strategy and Growth Opportunities with Leandro Cuccioli”, “Advertising with Sean Summers”, “Logistics with Agustin Costa”, and “Artificial Intelligence with Sebastian Barrios”, can be accessed here.  

About Mercado Libre

Founded in 1999, MercadoLibre, Inc is the leading company in e-commerce and financial technology in Latin America, with operations in 18 countries. It offers a complete ecosystem of solutions for individuals and businesses to buy, sell, advertise, obtain credit and insurance, collect, send money, save, and pay for goods and services both online and offline. Mercado Libre looks to facilitate access to commerce and financial services in Latin America, a market that offers great opportunities and high growth potential. It uses world-class technology to create intuitive solutions tailored to the local culture to transform the lives of millions of people in the region. More information at http://investor.mercadolibre.com/

PR BOND

Florencia Bonnin

flor@prbond.com

data.title: Ex-ASML employee in Dutch custody had contact with Russian intelligence, prosecutors say
data.description:
An ex-employee of major Dutch computer chip equipment maker ASML held on suspicion of stealing and selling corporate secrets to a Russian buyer also had contact with Russia's FSB intelligence service, prosecutors said on Thursday.

The Russian suspect, German Aksenov, has denied any wrongdoing. ASML and Dutch chip maker NXP , where he also worked, have said they do not believe Aksenov sold information that could have seriously harmed their businesses.

Dutch prosecutors said at a pre-trial hearing at which Aksenov's detention was extended that they had received information about his contact with the FSB via a message from Dutch intelligence agency AIVD in late December.

They did not elaborate on the import of the alleged contact but said the AIVD message had underlined that "gathering intelligence over science and technology is among the FSB's tasks".

Aksenov's lawyer Richard Jansen said outside the courtroom that he had agreed with his client to not yet respond to allegations made by the prosecution, including the alleged contact with the FSB, but they would give a full defence when the case comes to trial.

At the 43-year-old Aksenov's initial appearance in December, prosecutors said he had a contact named "Misha" who worked for a Russian firm called Innovative Engineering Centre and who was trying to set up a chip production plant in Russia.

Aksenov is suspected of stealing design manuals for microchips, microchip equipment and for technology with potential military applications that belonged to ASML, ASML subsidiary Mapper, NXP and the Delft University of Technology, to which he had access through his employment.

Prosecutors dropped money laundering and corruption charges against Aksenov at Thursday's hearing, saying their case would centre on the corporate theft and sanctions violations.

The prosecutors said there was a risk Aksenov might flee and would continue to leak secrets if he were freed from detention.

Aksenov spoke up to protest his jail conditions, citing limited contact with his partner and family in Russia.

He called it a "Catch 22" that he had lost both his apartment and his permission to remain in the Netherlands, but must remain in a Dutch jail.

Judge Annemiek Boer said she expected his conditions would be improved and set the next hearing in the case for April 4.

data.title: Tajikistan Cuts Key Rate to 8.75%
data.description:
The National Bank of Tajikistan lowered its key refinancing rate by 25 basis points to 8.75% during its February 2025 meeting, marking the first rate cut of the year.

This move follows three reductions in 2024, bringing borrowing costs to their lowest level since early 2016.

Policymakers cited stable inflation, which remained within the lower limit of the central bank’s target range, while closely monitoring domestic and global commodity prices.

By the end of 2024, annual inflation in Tajikistan had eased to 3.6%, down from 3.8% in 2023.

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Await Inventory Data — Market Talk — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. natural gas futures are lower ahead of the EIA's weekly storage report, but holding ground with some colder weather returning to mid-February forecasts. Natural gas storage likely fell by 170 Bcf to 2,401 Bcf in the last week of January, according to a Wall Street Journal survey, leaving stocks at a 107 Bcf deficit to the five-year average. The Nymex front month is down 1.3% at $3.317/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Hershey CEO Says High Cocoa Prices Driving Demand for Alternatives — Market Talk
data.description:
0949 ET - Cocoa alternatives are in growing demand given high prices for the real thing, Hershey CEO Michele Buck said. Some companies feeling the pressure have switched to cocoa butter alternatives, she said, reducing demand in the global cocoa market. "We do that where possible, but we're pretty precious about the brands and what they stand for with consumers, " Buck said. (roshan.fernandez@wsj.com)

0947 ET - A speech by Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem after the market closes will be eyed for possible freshened guidance. The speech, titled "Future Challenges for Monetary Policy in the Americas," will be closely watched for comments on heightened trade uncertainty, Scotiabank's Derek Holt says. "My hunch is that he may lean toward saying the damage has been done regardless of whether tariffs actually do arrive or not, but with mixed effects on the supply side (via reduced investment) and demand (exports)," the economist says in his morning report. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0938 ET - Jobless claims rebounded last week but still indicated a strong labor market, supporting a continued pause on interest rate cuts by the Fed, writes Gaurav Mallik, of Pallas Capital Advisors. With attention turning to tomorrow's employment report, Mallik says that as long as the economy added between 170,000 and 200,000 jobs in January, "the market should largely absorb this number with little volatility." Anything beyond these limits could spark a repricing of Fed expectations, which currently point to one or two rate cuts this year. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0936 ET - Canada's restaurant industry wants Ottawa to make its tax holiday permanent. Restaurants Canada estimates foodservice sales will grow 3.7% in 1Q thanks to a boost from the federal government's two-month removal of tax on restaurant meals, booze and certain other purchases. But the not-for-profit industry body says restaurants still have economic headwinds, including from cuts to immigration, still low consumer confidence and the tariff dispute with the U.S. It estimates restaurant, caterer and bar sales will surpass C$100 billion in 2025, representing a 3.9% nominal increase over 2024. But adjusted for menu inflation, real sales are forecast to grow by 0.8% compared to relatively flat real sales in 2024, it says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0924 ET - Industry outlooks for Europe and Switzerland look mixed, according to a survey of entrepreneurs released by UBS. As many as 52% of entrepreneurs in Europe and 50% in Switzerland are very or somewhat optimistic about the outlook for their industry over the next 12 months, the report says. In both Europe and Switzerland, companies are expected to invest in strategic acquisitions, IT spending, and personnel over the next 12 months, the study says. In Europe, investment into artificial intelligence is also anticipated, it says. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0921 ET - Sterling looks at risk of further falls after the Bank of England signalled less caution over cutting interest rates, TD Securities analysts say in a note. The BOE delivered a highly anticipated 25 basis-point rate cut Thursday but two members surprisingly backed a larger 50 basis-point reduction. Investors have now trimmed long sterling positions--bets on the currency rising--, the analysts say. However, sterling positioning remains "relatively optimistic" compared to non-dollar peers. This leaves it vulnerable to further declines. "Moreover, while U.K. does not seem to be in the obvious line of fire for Trump tariffs and can outperform the euro, it is a risk-sensitive currency and heightened uncertainty is likely to weigh on it versus the dollar." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0919 ET - The Czech koruna stays higher after the Czech National Bank delivered a widely anticipated interest-rate cut. The CNB resumed its rate-cutting cycle by lowering its key two-week repo rate by 25 basis points to 3.75%. Policymakers recently suggested a more cautious approach to monetary policy easing, Capital Economics economist Nicholas Farr says in a note. That means rate cuts from here are likely to be spaced out, he says. However, the prospect of continued slow economic growth and lower inflation could see rates fall to 3.0% by year-end. That compares to market expectations of 3.25%. EUR/CZK falls 0.4% on the day to 25.1149, compared to 25.1330 before the decision.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0905 ET - The 10-year Treasury yield remains below 4.5% amid continuing signs that the economy is gradually cooling. Weekly jobless claims, a proxy for layoffs, rise to 219,000 from a upwardly revised 208,000. Economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal expected 214,000. The four-week moving average increased by 4,000 to 216,750. January payrolls are due tomorrow and surveyed economists forecast a decrease to 169,000 from December's 256,000, with unemployment steady at 4.1%. The 10-year yield settled below 4.5% yesterday for the first time since December 18. It is now at 4.425%, while the two-year trades at 4.208%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0903 ET - Sterling's appreciation potential against the dollar looks limited after the Bank of England's latest policy decision, J.P. Morgan Private Bank strategist Matthew Landon says in a note. The BOE cut rates by 25 basis points to 4.5% Thursday but two of the nine members preferred a 50 basis-point reduction. The vote probably reflects concerns over a weaker growth outlook, Landon says. The market is now pricing the most rate cuts for this year since November's U.S. presidential election, he says. Given how weak growth is, greater policy divergence with the Federal Reserve seems justified. This will "likely keep a ceiling on how far the pound can appreciate against the dollar over the near-term." Sterling falls 0.9% to $1.2382. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0858 ET - The risks are probably skewed towards the Bank of England opting for limited further rate cuts this year, rather than increasing the pace of them, says Jessica Hinds at Fitch Ratings' economics team in a note. The BOE cut rates by 25 basis points to 4.50% but said in its statement that it sees persistent inflation as a bit more likely. Catherine Mann, normally the most hawkish MPC member, surprised with a call for a larger 50 basis-point reduction, but this doesn't necessarily signal a desire for large ongoing cuts. The MPC continues to emphasize a gradual approach to rate cuts which is more or less consistent with four cuts this year to 3.75%, as Fitch Ratings' economics team expects, she says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0846 ET - Two members of the Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee preferring a 50-basis-point interest-rate cut was a surprise and will "drive near-term headlines and market prices," says RBC BlueBay Asset Management's Neil Mehta. "We understand the concerns around weakening demand, given the direction of data following last autumn's budget," the portfolio manager says. However, reducing interest rates further risks increasing the probability of inflation persistence above the BOE's 2% target, he says, adding that core inflation is still running above 3% and wages above 5%. The BOE cut the bank rate by 25 basis points to 4.50%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0841 ET - The Bank of England's unanimous decision to cut interest rates on Thursday comes amid rising pressure to shore up the economy, Validus Risk Management analyst Pierre Roke says in a note. The BOE cut rates by 25 basis points to 4.5% although two officials voted in favor of a larger half-point cut. Recent data show zero economic growth. However, inflation is expected to remain above target through 2025. This indicates that the U.K. is under threat of stagflation, or weak growth combined with high inflation, Roke says. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: The Board of Directors of Swedbank utilises authorisation to acquire own shares to be used for employee compensation programmes
data.description:
Swedbank's Annual General Meeting 2024 decided to authorise the company'sBoardof Directors to resolve on the acquisition of own shares. Swedbank's Board ofDirectors has today decided to use this authorisation for the purpose ofensuring delivery of shares to participants in Swedbank's performance- andshare-based compensation programs (Eken 2024 and IP 2024, together "the Programs").

The acquisition of shares will be carried out in accordance with NasdaqStockholm's Nordic Main Market Rulebook for Issuers of Shares (the"Rulebook"),the Market Abuse Regulation (EU) No 596/2014 ("MAR"), and Commission DelegatedRegulation (EU) No 2016/1052 (the "Safe Harbour Regulation"). The acquisitionofshares will be managed by an investment firm or a credit institution makingitstrading decisions concerning the timing of the acquisitions independently ofSwedbank.

The buyback program is subject to the following conditions:

· Acquisitions may comprise up to 2,300,000 shares for a total amount notexceeding SEK 700 million, with the purpose of ensuring delivery of shares toparticipants in the Programs. However, Swedbank's holding of own shares mustnever exceed 10 percent of all shares in the company. · Acquisitions will only take place on Nasdaq Stockholm in accordance withtheRulebook, MAR, and the Safe Harbour Regulation. · The acquisition price per share shall be within the applicable price rangeon Nasdaq Stockholm at any given time, defined as the interval between thehighest bid price and lowest ask price continuously published by NasdaqStockholm, while also observing the limitations stipulated by the Safe HarbourRegulation. · Acquisitions may occur until the Annual General Meeting 2025. · Payment for shares shall be made in cash.

As of February 6, 2025, Swedbank's holding of own shares amounts to 6,686,779shares. The total number of shares issued by Swedbank is 1,132,005,722.

Contact:Hannes Mård (hannes.mard@swedbank.se), Media Relations Manager, tel: +46 7305741 95Magnus Alvesson (magnus.alvesson@swedbank.se), Head of Investor Relations,tel:+46 70 610 33 41

data.title: Wells Fargo Adjusts Price Target on Johnson Controls International to $98 From $92
data.description: Johnson Controls International has an average rating of overweight and mean price target of $93.79, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Oil Futures Recover Ground in Choppy Trade — Market Talk
data.description:
0950 ET - Oil futures are recouping some of the previous day's losses, while the market continues to ponder the impact of trade tariffs, rising U.S. crude stocks, and prospects of a step-up in OPEC+ output in April. "Most traders feel the U.S. tariff threats to China are for real and could damage their economy further, and with OPEC set to raise production soon, WTI prices may have heavier headwinds in the future," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Look for a choppy type trade until more is known about tariffs." WTI is up 0.7% at $71.53 a barrel, and Brent is 0.6% higher at $75.02.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0949 ET - Cocoa alternatives are in growing demand given high prices for the real thing, Hershey CEO Michele Buck said. Some companies feeling the pressure have switched to cocoa butter alternatives, she said, reducing demand in the global cocoa market. "We do that where possible, but we're pretty precious about the brands and what they stand for with consumers, " Buck said. (roshan.fernandez@wsj.com)

0947 ET - Steelmaker ArcelorMittal says it intends to build a plant in Alabama to produce the paper-thin steel used in the motors of electric vehicles, air conditioners and other products. Construction of the $1.2-billion plant at ArcelorMittal's steel mill near Mobile, Ala., will start later this year. Production is projected to begin in 2027. The plant will be able to produce about 165,000 tons a year. Production of electric vehicles is expected to drive higher demand for electrical steel, which accounts for about 1% of the worldwide steel production annually with most of it now in Asia. The only U.S. producers of electrical steel are Cleveland-Cliffs and U.S. Steel, which recently began production in Arkansas. (robert.tita@wsj.com )

0938 ET - Jobless claims rebounded last week but still indicated a strong labor market, supporting a continued pause on interest rate cuts by the Fed, writes Gaurav Mallik, of Pallas Capital Advisors. With attention turning to tomorrow's employment report, Mallik says that as long as the economy added between 170,000 and 200,000 jobs in January, "the market should largely absorb this number with little volatility." Anything beyond these limits could spark a repricing of Fed expectations, which currently point to one or two rate cuts this year. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0936 ET - Canada's restaurant industry wants Ottawa to make its tax holiday permanent. Restaurants Canada estimates foodservice sales will grow 3.7% in 1Q thanks to a boost from the federal government's two-month removal of tax on restaurant meals, booze and certain other purchases. But the not-for-profit industry body says restaurants still have economic headwinds, including from cuts to immigration, still low consumer confidence and the tariff dispute with the U.S. It estimates restaurant, caterer and bar sales will surpass C$100 billion in 2025, representing a 3.9% nominal increase over 2024. But adjusted for menu inflation, real sales are forecast to grow by 0.8% compared to relatively flat real sales in 2024, it says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0932 ET - Eli Lilly is slightly higher is early trading after another blowout quarter driven by GLP-1 drugs. 4Q revenue rises 45% to $13.53 billion. Revenue from new products climbs by $3.15 billion to $5.64 billion, driven Zepbound and Mounjaro. Lilly's fortunes echo those of Danish rival Novo Nordisk, which sold$4.71 billion of its Ozempic drug in 4Q. Lilly shares have more than quintupled in the last five years. Rival drug makers, such as Novartis, have admitted hunting for obesity drugs of their own. Lilly and Novo have major first-mover advantage, with smaller players such as Viking Therapeutics still playing catchup. Anya Schiess, a partner at JPMorgan's asset management unit, is among those who have predicted GLP-1s will be the best-selling class of drugs yet. (rob.curran@dowjones.com)

0914 ET - Tapestry's finance chief says the addition of an extra 10% tariff on goods made in China will have no material impact on the company's brands, which include Coach, Kate Spade and Stuart Weitzman. That is because Tapestry makes very few of its goods in China. Scott Roe, Tapestry's chief financial officer, says the company doesn't manufacture anything in Canada or Mexico.(Suzanne.Kapner@wsj.com)

0909 ET - To turn around Kate Spade, its parent company Tapestry plans to reduce choices for consumers. The number of handbag styles will decline 15% by the fall, Tapestry's chief executive Joanne Crevoiserat says. This will help the big ideas in leather goods cut through the clutter, she says. Kate Spade also plans to sharpen designs and elevate its handbag styles in an attempt to reverse persistent sales declines. Sales at the brand fell 10% in the most recent quarter. (Suzanne.Kapner@wsj.com)

0905 ET - The 10-year Treasury yield remains below 4.5% amid continuing signs that the economy is gradually cooling. Weekly jobless claims, a proxy for layoffs, rise to 219,000 from a upwardly revised 208,000. Economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal expected 214,000. The four-week moving average increased by 4,000 to 216,750. January payrolls are due tomorrow and surveyed economists forecast a decrease to 169,000 from December's 256,000, with unemployment steady at 4.1%. The 10-year yield settled below 4.5% yesterday for the first time since December 18. It is now at 4.425%, while the two-year trades at 4.208%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0900 ET - Western brands lost sales in their stores doing business in the Middle East after the outbreak of the Israel-Hamas war in October 2023, but some are seeing improvements. Fast food owner Yum Brands says transactions in its KFC restaurants in the Middle East rebounded in 4Q compared to before the conflict, with same-store sales in the region up 21%. Yum is up 3% premarket on a slight adjusted 4Q EPS beat. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

0853 ET - Value wars continue to play out among fast-food brands, making it harder for chains' individual deals to break through. Fast food brand owner Yum Brands says U.S. sales in its Pizza Hut division were under pressure in 4Q as competitors rolled out deals, and the chain's $7 promotions attracted existing customers but not infrequent ones. Yum says it will focus on promoting everyday good value at Pizza Hut in 2025, along with campaigns it hopes will break through. Pizza Hut's U.S. same-store sales declined 2% in 4Q. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

0847 ET - Ralph Lauren climbs 12% premarket after reporting an 11% increase in quarterly revenue and raising its outlook. Coach parent Tapestry also jumps this morning after reporting strong sales and raising guidance, showing that consumers are still snapping up expensive handbags and other goods--when the brands deliver what they want. (Suzanne.Kapner@wsj.com)

data.title: Natural Gas Edges Up on Cold Forecasts Ahead of Fresh Storage Data
data.description:
Natural gas edged higher ahead of fresh storage data as long-term forecasts expect colder than seasonal temperature are on the way.

Gas for March delivery was last seen up US$0.02 to US$3.38 per million British thermal units.

The rise comes as the National Weather Service expects most states to see colder than seasonal temperatures over its six to 10-day outlook, keeping heating demand high.

The Energy Information Administration will release its weekly report on inventories of the fuel later on Thursday morning, with a lighter than average draw on stocks expected.

"For today's EIA weekly storage report, survey averages suggest a draw of -168-171 Bcf, just under the 5-year average of -174 Bcf. It was colder vs normal over much of the US besides the Midwest," NatGasWeather noted.

data.title: Jobs Report Expected to Rock Markets if Expectations Are Missed — Market Talk
data.description: Jobless claims rebounded last week but still indicated a strong labor market, supporting a continued pause on interest rate cuts by the Fed, writes Gaurav Mallik, of Pallas Capital Advisors. With attention turning to tomorrow's employment report, Mallik says that as long as the economy added between 170,000 and 200,000 jobs in January, "the market should largely absorb this number with little volatility." Anything beyond these limits could spark a repricing of Fed expectations, which currently point to one or two rate cuts this year. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Dow Rises; Pound Sinks After Bank of England Cuts Rates — WSJ
data.description:
By Ed Ballard and Ryan Dezember

Chocolate bars and weight-loss drugs are both on the menu as a diverse range of companies report earnings.

Hershey, the confectioner, and Eli Lilly, which makes the anti-obesity drug Zepbound, both rose after updating investors this morning. There also were results from videogame platform Roblox and oil producer ConocoPhillips.

Meantime, Honeywell is in focus after the industrial conglomerate said it was preparing to split into three independent companies.

Amazon is set to report earnings after the bell Thursday. The company's cloud-services business will be scrutinized following disappointing results from Alphabet and Microsoft.

In recent trading:

Stock indexes rose slightly. The S&P 500 led the way, opening about 0.2% higher.

Benchmark Treasury yields ticked higher, after declining in the previous session. The 10-year yield settled at 4.421% Wednesday.

The dollar strengthened against most major currencies. The WSJ Dollar Index was up after a three-day decline.

The U.K. pound weakened after the Bank of England cut interest rates.

Overseas stocks climbed, led by Hong Kong's Hang Seng, which closed 1.4% higher, and China's Shanghai Composite, up 1.3%.

Write to Ed Ballard at ed.ballard@wsj.com and Ryan Dezember at ryan.dezember@wsj.com

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
